turns.user: is it possible to related Q(N) to trace of transfer matrix power ?
turns.note: User probes whether a standard trace formula (used for periodic BC systems) can be applied here; assumption-probe testing the transferability of a standard technique.
turns.assistant:
No — and the reason is exactly the nilpotency.

**The obstruction.** For any matrix $\mathcal{T}$, $\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{T}^k) = \sum_i \lambda_i^k$. Since every eigenvalue of $\mathcal{T}$ is zero ($\mathcal{T}^{N+1}=0$),

$$
\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{T}^k) \;=\; 0 \quad\text{for every } k\ge 1.
$$

So no expression of the form $\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{T}^N)$, $\mathrm{tr}(\mathcal{T}^{N+1})$, etc. can recover $Q(N)$ — they are all identically zero while $Q(N)>0$.

**Why the geometry forbids it.** A tr...

turns.user: is T hermitian ?
turns.note: Asking about a structural property of the transfer matrix; relevant to whether quantum-inspired methods (which rely on Hermiticity) apply.
turns.assistant:
No, and it can't be — there's a one-line argument.

**Argument from nilpotency.** $\mathcal{T}$ is nilpotent with every eigenvalue equal to zero. If $\mathcal{T}$ were Hermitian, its eigenvalues would be real and it would be diagonalizable in an orthonormal basis; a diagonalizable matrix whose eigenvalues are all zero is the zero matrix. turns.assistant:
$j$ is just the **kernel index** — it labels which of the several tensor kernels you're talking about.

**In plain CNN terms.** A standard convolutional layer has multiple filters (say 64 of them); you apply each filter to the same input and get 64 different output feature maps. $j$ is the same idea: it labels the kernels in a single TACNN layer, $j = 1, 2, \ldots, N_{\rm TK}$, where $N_{\rm TK}$ is the number of tensor kernels (the paper calls it "NTK" and sweeps it over $1, 2, 4, \ldots, 2048$...

turns.assistant:
I only have the abstract-level summary, not the full paper, so I can't give a definitive answer. But here's how to think about the question:

### What "real-time" means for QEC decoders

The decoder must output a correction **within one QEC cycle** — before the next round of syndrome measurements arrives. If it falls behind, syndromes queue up, errors accumulate uncorrected, and the logical qubit dies. The cycle time depends on the hardware platform:
